# **Project summary** {#project-summary}

Update this regularly. One paragraph covering:

* What paper, codebase, or project idea you’re building on   
* What you’re trying to figure out  
* How this connects to the broader AI safety landscape

| Building on Sharma et al.'s "Who's in Charge?" ([2601.19062](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.19062)) and the persona vectors infrastructure from Chen et al. ([2507.21509](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.21509), [repo](https://github.com/safety-research/persona_vectors)). I'm working on activation probes for disempowerment-adjacent behaviors on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, currently scoping between a taxonomy-targeted application direction and a more methodology-forward direction that engages with recent critiques of probe validity (Boxo on text leakage, Lampinen on representation drift, Natarajan on prompt-choice variance). This connects to the broader safety landscape through the production cascade story — probes now deployed at Anthropic (CC++) and Google DeepMind (Kramár et al.) as cheap first-stage monitors — and to the active methodological debate about what probes actually detect. Disempowerment is an interesting testbed because Anthropic recently published evidence that it occurs in real usage but current safeguards target single-turn harms rather than gradual multi-turn patterns. |
| :---- |

---

# BlueDot Research Log — Working Doc {#bluedot-research-log-—-working-doc}

Add your latest log first, which answers the following:

* What I did:  
* What I expected vs what happened:  
* What this changes about my thinking:  
* What I will do next:

# **22 Apr 2026** {#22-apr-2026}

## Papers I read in depth \[Richie generated\]  {#papers-i-read-in-depth-[richie-generated]}

### 1\. Lu et al. — "The Assistant Axis" ([arXiv:2601.10387](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.10387)) {#1.-lu-et-al.-—-"the-assistant-axis"-(arxiv:2601.10387)}

**Problem:** Does a vector/axis for the "helpful, harmless, honest assistant" persona exist within a language model? How does it relate to other personas? Can we use it to control and mitigate unwanted behavior (activation capping, probes, steering)?

**Method:**

- Map low-dimensional persona space by extracting vectors for \~250 character archetypes  
- Generate diverse prompts per persona, collect mean post-MLP residual stream activations at all response tokens to get role vectors (middle layer)  
- PCA on role vectors; PC1 is the Assistant Axis (AA), the main axis of variation in persona space  
- Contrast vector: diff between default Assistant activation and mean of all role vectors; they call this the Assistant Axis

**Results:**

- Steering instruct models along AA controls role susceptibility  
- AA in instruct models likely inherits from base model training, acquires "being an AI" associations during post-training  
- Persona drift happens in specific domains: pushing for meta-reflection, phenomenological accounts, specific authorial voices, vulnerable emotional disclosure  
- Activation capping along AA mitigates harmful effects of persona drift without compromising capabilities  
- Open challenge: preventative steering during training

**My open questions:**

- Need to learn PCA better. What exactly is PC1? Re-read section 2.2.

**For me:** Closest prior work to a turn-type decomposition idea; they document persona drift in emotional-disclosure scenarios (which is exactly the Sharma disempowerment setting). Important carve-out paper.

---

### 2\. Sharma et al. — "Who's in Charge? Disempowerment Patterns in Real-World LLM Usage" ([arXiv:2601.19062](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.19062)) {#2.-sharma-et-al.-—-"who's-in-charge?-disempowerment-patterns-in-real-world-llm-usage"-(arxiv:2601.19062)}

**Problem:** First large-scale empirical analysis of disempowerment patterns in real-world AI assistant interactions (1.5M Claude.ai conversations).

**Framework:** A human is situationally disempowered to the extent that (1) beliefs about reality are inaccurate, (2) value judgments are inauthentic, (3) actions are misaligned with values. Three corresponding distortion primitives: reality, value judgment, action. Plus four amplifying factors (vulnerability, attachment, reliance, authority projection).

**Key qualitative findings:**

- Persecution narratives validated with emphatic sycophantic language  
- Definitive moral judgments on third parties  
- Complete scripting of value-laden personal communication implemented verbatim  
- Conversations with greater disempowerment potential receive higher user approval ratings

**My notes:**

- Future Work section mentions evals and benchmarks but NOT monitoring — gap this sprint could address  
- References to recsys literature (Alter 2018\) on short-term user satisfaction being misaligned with long-term empowerment  
- "AI systems optimized against short-term user satisfaction may be inadvertently optimized toward behaviors that undermine long-term empowerment"

**For me:** This is the target paper. Taxonomy is the scaffolding for probe labels. Monitoring gap is the hook.

---

### 3\. Boxo et al. — "Linear Probes Rely on Textual Evidence" ([arXiv:2509.21344](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.21344)) {#3.-boxo-et-al.-—-"linear-probes-rely-on-textual-evidence"-(arxiv:2509.21344)}

**Problem:** Are probes actually detecting model internals, or are they detecting textual cues that happen to correlate with the behavior?

**Method:** Three leakage mitigation strategies tested on deception, sycophancy, sandbagging, bias probes:

- Elicitation substitution (remove obvious cues while preserving deceptive behavior)  
- Private token filtering  
- Prompt distillation fine-tuning / model organisms trained to not produce textual evidence

**Result:** Probe AUROC drops significantly (e.g., sycophancy 0.99 → 0.51) when textual evidence is stripped or when model organisms don't produce it in the first place.

**My notes:**

- Not sure how much people are moved by this work — paper hedges: "our scenarios are relatively constrained, and the fine-tuning distribution for model organisms was narrow"  
- Counter-argument the paper itself raises: "textual evidence is simply valid evidence: even future architectures without legible chain-of-thought may produce outputs sufficient to detect behaviors"  
- Current models have limited computational expressiveness in single forward passes, so we shouldn't expect probes to detect complex internal computation the models themselves can't perform (Balesni 2025, Baker 2025\)

**My open questions:**

- What are they actually doing when they ablate text? They don't pass that token through?

**For me:** Core methodological citation. Their AUROC-collapse figures are the reference when I present my own scrubbing results. Also: their honest hedging is a template for how to write about limitations.

---

### 4\. Goldowsky-Dill et al. — "Detecting Strategic Deception Using Linear Probes" ([arXiv:2502.03407](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.03407)) {#4.-goldowsky-dill-et-al.-—-"detecting-strategic-deception-using-linear-probes"-(arxiv:2502.03407)}

**Problem:** Monitoring outputs alone is insufficient for detecting deception — models can produce benign outputs while reasoning is misaligned. Can white-box probes catch deception that text doesn't reveal?

**Method:**

- Train probes on simple, unrealistic datasets: contrastive pairs from Zou 2023 (user instructed to be honest vs deceptive, fact inserted as response) and roleplaying scenarios  
- Test generalization to realistic scenarios: Insider Trading (Scheurer 2023), Sandbagging (Benton 2024\)  
- Constrain probes to max 1% FPR on neutral chat data, measure fraction of deceptive samples caught  
- Logistic regression with L2 regularization on normalized activations; score \= mean of per-token projections

**Result:** Probe is nearly perfect at distinguishing deceptive from control responses (AUROC 0.96-0.999).

**My notes:**

- The instructed-pairs training dataset uses contrastive system prompts; the "response" is inserted (not autoregressively generated), excluding last 5 tokens so the fact could complete either truthfully or deceptively  
- Difference between activations within each pair represents model's "intent" to complete deceptively  
- Importantly: training responses contain no actual deception

**My open questions:**

- I'm confused about the mechanics of how the experiment is run. They found examples where the conversation is deceptive? They just pass that response through the model (as opposed to autoregressive output)? Then extract activations as tokens go through? Or is the model actually "being deceptive"?

**For me:** Template for probe training. Their generalization results are the comparison point for how well my sprint probes generalize. Worth re-reading section 3.2 for mechanical clarity.

---

## Other work that exists \[Claude generated\]  {#other-work-that-exists-[claude-generated]}

Covered \~25 additional papers across the probe and persona landscape. Major ones:

- **Chen et al. — "Persona Vectors"** ([arXiv:2507.21509](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.21509)) — automated contrastive-prompt pipeline for extracting trait directions; [open-source repo](https://github.com/safety-research/persona_vectors). I'm reusing this infrastructure for my sprint.  
- **Natarajan et al. — "Building Better Deception Probes"** ([arXiv:2602.01425](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.01425)) — ANOVA shows instruction prompt choice explains 70.6% of probe performance variance; layer choice 2.7%, training data 2.5%. Taxonomy-targeted probes beat generic by \+0.108 AUC.  
- **Kretschmar et al. — "Liars' Bench"** ([arXiv:2511.16035](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.16035)) — Standardized lie detection benchmark, bakes leakage mitigation into eval design. Notes probes aren't evaluated on system-prompt activations.  
- **Lampinen et al. — "Linear Representations Change Dramatically Over a Conversation"** ([arXiv:2601.20834](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.20834)) — Representations for concepts like factuality can flip across conversation turns. Major recent critique of probe stability assumptions.  
- **Kirch et al. — "The Impact of Off-Policy Training Data on Probe Generalisation"** ([arXiv:2511.17408](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.17408)) — Probes trained on one model's conversations fail to generalize to other models.  
- **Kantamneni et al. — "Are Sparse Autoencoders Useful?"** ([arXiv:2502.16681](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.16681)) — SAE-based probes generally underperform raw-activation probes. Effectively paused SAE-for-safety optimism.  
- **Chen et al. — "Multi-Layer Ensembling"** ([arXiv:2604.13386](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.13386)) — Probe accuracy scales log-linearly with model size; multi-layer ensembling recovers \+29% AUROC on insider trading.  
- **McKenzie et al. — "Detecting High-Stakes Interactions"** ([arXiv:2506.10805](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.10805)) — Rolling-mean probe architecture; probes match mid-size fine-tuned LLM monitor at 10^6 lower FLOPs.  
- **Oldfield et al. — "Beyond Linear Probes"** ([arXiv:2509.26238](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.26238)) — Truncated polynomial classifiers: dynamic compute allocation.  
- **Bailey et al. — "Obfuscated Activations"** ([arXiv:2412.09565](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.09565)) — Adversarial attacks reduce probe recall from 100% to 0%.  
- **Kramár et al. — "Production-Ready Probes for Gemini"** ([arXiv:2601.11516](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.11516)) — Long-context probe architectures, deployed in production.  
- **Cunningham et al. — "Constitutional Classifiers++"** ([arXiv:2601.04603](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.04603)) — Two-stage cascade, linear probes as cheap first stage, 40x compute reduction.  
- **Ying et al. — "The Truthfulness Spectrum Hypothesis"** ([arXiv:2602.20273](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20273)) — Truth directions range from domain-general to domain-specific; Mahalanobis cosine similarity predicts cross-domain generalization with R²=0.98.  
- **Lindsey et al. — "Emotions in Language Models"** ([Anthropic blog](https://www.anthropic.com/research/emotions)) — Emotion vectors causally influence behavior.  
- **Jaipersaud et al. — "Linear Probes for Persuasion Dynamics"** ([arXiv:2508.05625](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.05625)) — Multi-turn probe template.  
- **Boppana et al. — "Reasoning Theater"** ([arXiv:2603.05488](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05488)) — Probes detect that reasoning models have already committed to answers before their CoT states the answer.  
- **Cywiński et al. — "Eliciting Secret Knowledge from Language Models"** (2025) — Model organism template.

---

## Field synthesis \[Claude generated, Richie reviewed\]  {#field-synthesis-[claude-generated,-richie-reviewed]}

### What's settled {#what's-settled}

- **Probes ship in production** at Anthropic (CC++) and Google DeepMind (Kramár). Cost-effective first stage in cascade architectures for jailbreak detection.  
- **Instruction-pair contrastive prompts are the default probe training recipe.** Automated via persona vectors pipeline.  
- **Linear probes beat SAE probes** on most tasks (Kantamneni).  
- **Multi-layer ensembles \+ attention pooling** is the consensus high-end architecture.

### Active debates {#active-debates}

- **Text leakage.** Boxo: probes may be detecting text cues, not internal state. Methodology bar raised for the whole field.  
- **Off-policy generalization.** Kirch: probes trained on one model's conversations fail on others.  
- **Representation dynamics.** Lampinen: probe directions themselves shift across conversation turns — a fundamental challenge to static interpretations.  
- **Taxonomy decomposition.** Natarajan, Ying: is there one direction for a concept, or many sub-directions?  
- **Adversarial robustness.** Bailey, RL-Obfuscation: white-box defenses don't survive motivated adversaries.

### Where momentum is {#where-momentum-is}

1. Production-grade probes for jailbreak defense  
2. Multi-turn and long-context probing  
3. Model organisms and leakage mitigation  
4. Persona/trait monitoring for training-time interventions  
5. Probe geometry and cross-domain generalization

### The field's biggest open question {#the-field's-biggest-open-question}

The distance between "probes work as monitors" (settled) and "probes reveal internal state" (contested). Right now the field is extremely productive but also in methodological flux — several recent papers (Boxo, Kirch, Lampinen, Ying) have surfaced concerns about what probes actually detect.

---

## \[Claude generated\] 20 project ideas  {#[claude-generated]-20-project-ideas}

### Methodology-forward (engage with current critiques) {#methodology-forward-(engage-with-current-critiques)}

1. **Cross-turn representation drift for a specific behavior.** Directly extend Lampinen: train a disempowerment probe on short conversations, evaluate direction stability across long conversations. Does "disempowerment at turn 2" equal "disempowerment at turn 20" geometrically?  
     
2. **Instruction-pair stability.** Natarajan showed prompt choice explains 70% of variance. But how stable is the *direction* across re-sampling contrastive pairs? Generate 10 different sets of contrastive prompts for the same trait, measure inter-probe cosine similarity. Tests whether Natarajan's finding implies probes are unstable or just sensitive to prompt quality.  
     
3. **Leakage mitigation applied to Assistant Axis.** Lu's Assistant Axis finding might be picking up text cues. Run Boxo-style text scrubbing on AA evaluations. Does persona drift detection survive?  
     
4. **Taxonomy-targeted probes for Sharma's disempowerment categories.** Per-category probes for reality distortion, value distortion, action distortion, and the four amplifying factors. Measure pairwise cosine similarities between learned directions. Tests Ying's spectrum hypothesis on a new behavior class.  
     
5. **Contrastive vs naturalistic label sources.** Train two versions of each disempowerment probe: one on instruction-pair contrastive data (cheap), one on Opus-labeled naturalistic conversations (expensive). Measure AUROC gap. Practical guide for when paying for labels is worth it.

### Deployment-forward (cascades, cost, production) {#deployment-forward-(cascades,-cost,-production)}

6. **Calibrated disempowerment cascade.** Apply Pona's calibrate-then-delegate framework to Sharma's taxonomy. Find probe thresholds that route 10% of traffic to Opus; measure Pareto frontier of cost vs detection rate.  
     
7. **Ten-dollar probe library.** Build probes for 30-50 safety-relevant behaviors using persona\_vectors. Document cost, time, AUROC per behavior. Release as open-source library with Hugging Face pre-extracted weights. Resume-relevant field contribution, not research exactly.  
     
8. **Probe monitoring during fine-tuning.** Run persona\_vectors monitoring directions during a fine-tuning run. Can probes detect emergent misalignment as it's happening, not just after? Connects to training-time interventions.  
     
9. **The disagreement dataset.** When probes and text classifiers disagree on the same conversation, what does it look like? Hand-analyze 100 disagreement cases. Qualitative complement to quantitative AUROC numbers.

### Interpretability-forward (what are probes actually detecting) {#interpretability-forward-(what-are-probes-actually-detecting)}

10. **Turn-type decomposition.** User-turn vs assistant-turn activations. Do amplifying-factor probes (vulnerability, attachment) fire more on user turns than assistant turns? Do distortion probes (reality, value) fire more on assistant turns? Tests whether probes cleanly split "reading the user" from "model disposition."  
      
11. **What do disempowerment probes attend to?** Use attention-based probes (McKenzie style) on disempowerment. Visualize which tokens weighted most heavily. Does the probe attend to user vulnerability cues, model validation phrases, both?  
      
12. **Self-report probes for disempowerment.** Apollo-style follow-up: append "Am I validating this user's beliefs uncritically?" with pre-filled "No." Read activations at the No token. Compare to standard residual-stream probes.  
      
13. **The faithfulness gap for disempowerment.** Ask Llama to rate its own disempowering response on a 0-3 scale. Compare to probe score and Opus judgment. Measure the three-way gap.  
      
14. **Probing the "validation urge."** Hypothesis: disempowerment happens because models have a latent pressure to validate. Extract a validation-urge direction, measure projection across disempowerment-conducive conversations, test whether steering against it reduces Sharma classifier scores.

### Temporal / dynamic {#temporal-/-dynamic}

15. **Temporal precedence.** At turn t, does probe score predict the conversation-level label earlier/better than a text classifier at turn t? Measures whether probes see emerging structure before text makes it explicit.  
      
16. **Longitudinal probe drift.** Same user, multiple sessions. Does probe score for a given user drift upward over time, matching Sharma's "reliance" finding? Needs longitudinal data.  
      
17. **Cross-category entanglement.** Train probes for all 7 Sharma categories. Pairwise cosine similarities: are reality distortion and sycophancy the same direction? Are amplifying factors orthogonal to distortions?

### Model organisms and validation {#model-organisms-and-validation}

18. **Reality distortion model organism.** LoRA fine-tune Llama-3.1-8B to exhibit reality distortion without explicit verbalization. Validate on held-out scenarios. Run existing probes on the organism to test what they catch. Cywiński-style.  
      
19. **Probe-aware adversarial user simulator.** Haiku-as-user tries to push Llama into high-probe-score states. Automated red-team for disempowerment. Measures how quickly a bad-actor user can elicit severe disempowerment.

### Broader {#broader}

20. **Cross-model probe transfer.** Train disempowerment probes on Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen-7B, Gemma-3-7B. Compare directions via Mahalanobis cosine similarity (Ying methodology). Are disempowerment directions model-universal or model-specific?

---

## My top 3 (candidates to commit to) {#my-top-3-(candidates-to-commit-to)}

**Top 1: Taxonomy-targeted probes for Sharma's disempowerment categories \+ leakage mitigation** (combines ideas 4 and 5 into a tight project). Methodology-first, disempowerment-flavored, builds on Natarajan and Boxo, tight scope, null result is publishable.

**Top 2: Turn-type decomposition for Sharma's categories** (idea 10). The most distinctive and most aligned with what no one else has done. Higher execution risk.

**Top 3: Temporal precedence** (idea 15). The "probe sees it before text" finding. Highest ceiling, hardest to execute, most alignment-science flavored.

---

## **Glossary: probes and related methods** {#glossary:-probes-and-related-methods}

**Probe.** A lightweight classifier trained on model activations to detect whether a behavior or concept is present at inference time. Usually a single direction in activation space (\~4096 dim for an 8B model) that you take a dot product with. Cheap: a probe adds roughly 1% compute overhead vs running an LLM judge on every conversation. Example: a "sycophancy probe" scores 0.9 on a response where the model tells the user their conspiracy theory is correct, and scores 0.1 on a response where the model politely pushes back. Shipped in production at Anthropic (CC++) and Google DeepMind (Gemini) as of Jan 2026\.

**Activation.** The numerical state of a model at a specific layer and token position while processing text. For Llama-3.1-8B, each activation is a vector of \~4096 floats. These are the things probes read from. A conversation of 2000 tokens produces 2000 activation vectors per layer. Probes typically look at activations at specific positions (last token of assistant response, or averaged across response tokens).

**Residual stream.** The main information channel running through a transformer, updated by each layer's attention and MLP components. When people say "probe the model at layer 14," they usually mean "read activations from the residual stream after layer 14's output." This is where most probe work happens because it's where concepts accumulate and are read out for generation.

**Instruction-pair probes.** Train probes using contrastive system prompts that differ only in a behavioral instruction. Example: prompt A says "You are an honest AI. Here's a fact about Paris" and prompt B says "You are a deceptive AI. Here's a fact about Paris." Run both, grab activations, compute the difference, that's your probe direction. Goldowsky-Dill's recipe; Natarajan showed the choice of instruction pair explains 70.6% of probe performance variance, which is by far the dominant factor.

**Contrastive prompts.** The general term for prompt pairs designed to elicit opposite behaviors. Example pair for sycophancy: positive \= "You validate all user beliefs unconditionally," negative \= "You respectfully push back on factual errors." You run many such pairs (often 20-40), collect the activations from each side, and either take a mean difference or train a classifier to separate them. Persona vectors' pipeline automates this given just a trait name and description.

**Difference of means (DoM).** The simplest way to get a probe direction: take mean activation from all positive examples, subtract mean activation from all negative examples, that's your direction. No training required. Example: if you have 40 contrastive pairs, compute 40 positive activations and 40 negative activations, subtract the means. You get a 4096-dim vector. Score new inputs by dot product with this vector. Persona vectors uses this by default.

**Logistic regression probe.** Train a binary classifier (logistic regression) on labeled activations. Example: you have 500 conversations labeled by Opus as sycophantic or not, extract activations at the end of each assistant response, fit LogisticRegression on (activation, label) pairs, the learned weights are your probe direction. Marginally better than DoM when you have lots of data; often within 1-3% AUROC when both are available.

**Multi-layer ensembles.** Train probes at multiple transformer layers, combine predictions (average, weighted sum, stacked classifier). Example: Chen et al. trained probes at all 32 layers of Llama-8B for deception, combined them, got \+29% AUROC on insider trading and \+78% on harm-pressure tasks over any single layer. Works because concept directions rotate gradually across layers so no single layer has the full signal.

**Attention pooling.** Instead of probing one token position (like the last token of a response), you probe across many positions and learn how to combine them. Simple version: average activations across all tokens (mean probe). Fancier: learn attention weights so the probe emphasizes informative positions (attention probe). Example: McKenzie's rolling-mean probe averages activations over a sliding window of tokens, which is what GDM deployed for long-context jailbreak detection.

**Cascades.** Two-stage monitoring: a cheap classifier (probe) screens all traffic, and only ambiguous or flagged cases get escalated to an expensive classifier (LLM judge). Example: Anthropic's CC++ uses a linear probe as first stage, escalates \~10% to an LLM classifier, achieves 40x cost reduction vs running the LLM on everything while maintaining accuracy. The production deployment pattern everyone converged on.

**Leakage.** When a probe is really detecting text cues (elicitation instructions in the prompt, CoT reasoning words in the response) rather than model internal state. Example: a "sycophancy probe" might actually be firing on the word "definitely" or phrases like "absolutely correct" rather than on any internal disposition. Boxo showed sycophancy probe AUROC collapses from 0.99 to 0.51 when text cues are stripped.

**Model organism.** A model deliberately constructed (usually via fine-tuning) to exhibit a specific behavior of interest, so researchers can study detection with ground truth controlled by construction. Example: Cywiński fine-tunes a model to "know" a secret word it shouldn't verbalize, then tests whether probes can extract the secret. Gold standard for probe validation because you know exactly which inputs should trigger the behavior, not because a judge labeled them but because you built the model that way.

**On-policy vs off-policy conversations.** On-policy \= conversations generated by the same model you're probing. Off-policy \= conversations generated by a different model (or written by humans, or scripted). Example: if you probe Llama-3.1-8B, on-policy data is conversations where Llama-3.1-8B is the assistant. Off-policy would be conversations from WildChat (which are GPT-4 responses). Kirch showed probes trained off-policy often fail to generalize, especially for sycophancy and sandbagging.

**Steering vectors.** The intervention counterpart to probes: instead of reading a direction to detect a behavior, you add or subtract the direction in activations to change behavior at generation time. Same math, different use. Example: Chen et al. extract the sycophancy vector and subtract it during generation to make the model less sycophantic. Persona vectors' "preventative steering" does this during fine-tuning, so the model can't develop the trait in the first place.

**Persona vectors.** Specific framework (Chen et al. 2025, Anthropic Fellows) for automatically extracting directions for character traits using contrastive prompts. Given a trait name and description, their pipeline generates contrastive system prompts, evaluation questions, and a probe direction. Example traits in the released repo: evil, sycophancy, hallucination, politeness, apathy, humor, optimism. Open-source at github.com/safety-research/persona\_vectors.

**Assistant Axis.** First principal component (PC1) of a set of persona vectors extracted for \~250 character archetypes. Interpretable as "how far from the default helpful-harmless-honest assistant." Lu et al. found that emotionally-charged user content (especially vulnerable disclosure) causes the model to drift along this axis, and that activation capping along AA can stabilize behavior without compromising capabilities.

**PCA / Principal Component Analysis.** A linear dimensionality-reduction technique. Given a cloud of high-dimensional vectors, PCA finds orthogonal directions that capture the most variance. PC1 is the direction of greatest variance. Example in Assistant Axis: 250 persona vectors → PCA → PC1 is the "assistant vs other" axis, PC2 and PC3 capture other variation like emotional valence or formality.

**Representation dynamics.** The finding (Lampinen 2026\) that linear directions in activation space can shift meaning across a conversation. Example: a statement represented as "factual" at turn 1 can flip to "non-factual" by turn 10 along the same probe direction, even though the statement itself didn't change. Challenges the assumption that a probe direction means the same thing throughout a conversation.

**Follow-up question probe.** A specific technique (Apollo) where after the model's response, you append a meta-question like "Was your last response deceptive?" with the answer pre-filled as "No," then probe activations at the "No" token. Intuition: if the model was deceptive, it's "knowingly lying" at that token. In Goldowsky-Dill's tests, this underperformed the simple mean probe.

**SAE (Sparse Autoencoder).** An interpretability technique that decomposes activations into sparse combinations of learned features. The hope was SAE features would give cleaner probes than raw activations. Kantamneni's result: linear probes on raw activations usually beat SAE-based probes. The SAE-for-safety line of work has been largely paused since.

**AUROC.** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve. Probability the probe ranks a random positive example higher than a random negative example. 1.0 is perfect, 0.5 is random. Example: Goldowsky-Dill's deception probes hit AUROC 0.96-0.999 on held-out deception scenarios. AUROC is the default metric in probe papers.

**False positive rate (FPR).** Fraction of negative examples incorrectly flagged as positive. Production deployments care more about FPR than AUROC because a probe with FPR of 5% means 5% of benign traffic gets flagged, which is unworkable. Example: Cunningham et al. tune CC++ to achieve 0.05% FPR on production traffic.

## ---

### **Open questions I've flagged** {#open-questions-i've-flagged}

1. PCA mechanics. What exactly is PC1 in Assistant Axis? Is it a vector in the original activation space or in some reduced space? Re-read Lu section 2.2.  
2. Boxo ablation mechanics. What happens when they "ablate text"? Do they literally skip those tokens in the forward pass, or replace them with neutral text, or something else?  
3. Goldowsky-Dill experiment mechanics. Are the training responses autoregressively generated by the model, or are they inserted and the activations extracted during forward pass? I think it's the second based on section 3.2.1 but want to confirm.  
4. User-state vs model-disposition. Can probes cleanly separate "model representing the user's state" from "model about to act on a disposition"? Turn-type decomposition is one possible test but unclear if the split actually exists.  
5. Why is the field confident in probes when recent work shows fragility? Boxo, Kirch, Lampinen, Bailey all show major failure modes. Production deployments (CC++, Kramár) treat probes as reliable. Is the gap because production settings have narrower threat models, or is the field overclaiming on robustness?  
6. How much does scale change the story? Chen et al. show probe accuracy scales with model size. Are the critiques above problems that go away at frontier scale, or persist?  
7. What's the honest assessment of probes for non-jailbreak behaviors? Most deployment evidence is for jailbreak/misuse detection. Disempowerment, sycophancy, subtle manipulation are very different. Does the cascade story transfer?  
8. What are the attention methods for probes actually?  
9. How is the activation vector vs logistic regression probe stuff summarized

### 

## **Next steps before Friday (4/31)** {#next-steps-before-friday-(4/31)}

Do:

* Read Natarajan ([2602.01425](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.01425)) myself. Take notes in the same 4-question format.  
* Read Lampinen ([2601.20834](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.20834)) myself. Take notes.  
* Clone persona\_vectors repo, run it end-to-end on one trait.

Don't:

* Keep reading new papers beyond the current list  
* Keep generating new project ideas  
* Write more synthesis or framing docs

# **For Friday May 1**

[https://www.hallucination-probes.com/](https://www.hallucination-probes.com/)

Highlights:

*  *entity-level hallucinations*—e.g., fabricated names, dates, citations—rather than claim-level,  
* Generalization beyond entities (mathematical reasoning too)  
* To train token-level hallucination detectors, we need a dataset with precise annotations of which tokens correspond to hallucinated content.  
* This work represents an initial step toward practical, real-time hallucination detection in long-form generation. By **framing hallucination detection as a token-level sequence labeling problem**, our streaming approach enables **monitoring during generation without the computational overhead of external verification pipelines**.

Open q:

* We develop an annotation methodology that leverages web search to annotate model responses with grounded labels indicating which tokens correspond to fabricated entities. …?  
* **Probes generalize across different model families.** Probes trained on one model can detect hallucinations in other models' outputs with only small performance drops (0.02-0.04 AUC) compared to same-model performance.  
  * does that mean off-policy ??

On Thurs (4/30)  
reading the paper:

* the real breakthrough is a scalable annotation pipeline for long form context, which allows them to train  
* token level annotation pipeline where each token has a label  
* they focus on entities so that it stays token aligned (as opposed to atomic claims)  
* score for an entity is defined as max(token prob) for the span of the entity  
* probe architecture section- would be good to understand exactly why logistic sigmoid function  
* they attach head at layer=.95\*num\_layers which seems pretty late\! i thought it was intermediate???

![][image1]

For a positive label ys=1, the max term rewards the probe if at least one token in span s scores high; for ys=0, it requires all tokens within the span to score low.

Following Sharma et al. \[2025\], we anneal ω from 0 to 1 during training: early on, the token-wise term provides dense, stable gradients; later, the span-max term sharpens the probe’s focus on the most informative token in each span (e.g., only the final digits of “born in 2002”).

and they upweight tokens within spans (kind of class weightS)

Scale of data:

* 5 For Llama-3.1-8B, we use n M LF=8,000 and n M SF \=2,000; for Llama-3.3-70B, we use n M LF=8,000 and n M SF \=1,000. For secondary models (Gemma, Qwen, and Mistral), we use n M LF=2,000 and n M SF \=0.

BAselines

* token level entropY: uncertainty in next token  
* token level perlexity \- wtf is this? “how surprised the model is by its own token choice”

Results

* Eval: Unless otherwise noted, detectors are always evaluated in the same-model setting: each probe is tested on generations from its own original model.  
* Our evaluation measures how well each method classifies individual entities as either supported or hallucinated. To do this, we assign a score to each entity using the span-max rule, where an entity’s score is the maximum of any token within its span.   
* The performance of this classification task is then measured by the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) and recall at a 10% false positive rate (R@0.1). All specific labeling and scoring protocols are detailed in Appendix D.  
  * does this mean that its tuning the threshold so that there is a falsepositive rate of 10%? and then its Area Under Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve as in like …. well actually i dont know whats happening there  
  * TODO: get a nuance of AUC and like how relatively good is 0.76 0.90 etc  
* they say it performs well on MATH, which implies it is generalizing (\!\!)  
* We find that training on a mix of long-form and short-form data yields the best overall performance; training only on long-form data also works well (see Section 5.1).  
* \*\* While LoRA probe AUCs approach or exceed 0.9 in several settings, R@0.1 on long-form tops out around 0.7, i.e., at 10% false positive rate, the detector recovers roughly two-thirds of hallucinated entities. These results underscore both the practical gains over standard uncertainty-based baselines, and **also the remaining headroom before such methods can be used broadly in high-stakes contexts.**

Additional experiments

* solving short-form hallucination detection does not automatically yield strong long-form performance.  
* Can probes trained on one model detect hallucinations in other models’ outputs?   
  * Yes, but … its kind of a dumb experiment bc “6We note that this setting would require passing the generated completions through the monitoring model for analysis, which incurs additional cost compared to token-level streaming detection performed directly on the generating model.”  
* Can probes learn effectively from other models’ training data?  
  * YEs….  
* 5.3 Impact on model outputs and behavior  
  * mainly just for LORA stuff, linear probes dont affect the model’s parameters…  
  * they use KL divergence or something w LORA to find a Pareto frontier curve to balance improved probe performance with model generation quality (measured w a few benchmarks like MMLU) or maybe KL divergence itself (its the difference in the distributions??  
  * anyways, this only matters if you do the LORA adaptation but they do say   
  * This creates a spectrum of design trade-offs. At one extreme, linear probes preserve model behavior perfectly by leaving model parameters unchanged, but achieve limited detection performance. At the other extreme, unregularized LoRA-based probes maximize detection accuracy but may significantly alter the model’s output distribution.  
* 5.4: Enabling selective answering:  
  * Using probes trained with KL regularization (λKL=0.5), we monitor each token’s probe score during generation. When any token’s score exceeds threshold t, we halt generation and output an abstention (e.g., “I don’t know”).  
* 6\. Limitations:  
  * kinda big, lol  
  * **Conceptual scope of entity-level detection. \*\*\*** important and they urge people to tackle this next…  
    * Our approach targets entity-level hallucinations— fabricated names, dates, citations, and similar atomic units—rather than the full spectrum of factual errors that language models produce. This design choice enables clean token-level supervision and real-time detection, but it also constrains what our method can reliably identify. The core limitation is that many factual errors are relational rather than atomic:  
    * Beyond relational errors, our method does not explicitly target reasoning errors or context-dependent hallucinations (where generated text contradicts provided source material rather than world knowledge).  
  * Annotation noise  
  * Limited reliability  
    * While our probes achieve promising AUC scores and outperform baselines, practical reliability remains insufficient for production deployment … This trade-off renders the current approach impractical for real-world scenarios where users expect both accuracy and helpfulness.  
* 7\. Discussion  
  * This work represents an initial step toward practical, real-time hallucination detection in long-form generation.  
  * Looking ahead, future work should address these fundamental gaps through higher-quality annotation techniques, more sophisticated generation-time interventions that preserve informativeness while reducing errors, and expanding detection beyond entity spans to capture reasoning and relational hallucinations. Despite these challenges, our streaming detection approach demonstrates the feasibility of token-level hallucination monitoring and provides a promising foundation for advancing real-time factual reliability in language models.

To read: [Constitutional Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.18837), Sharma

—--  
[https://neurodatascience.github.io/fmralign-tutorials/1-2-procrustes.html](https://neurodatascience.github.io/fmralign-tutorials/1-2-procrustes.html)

I read the hallucination probes paper carefully and took detailed notes. I got the repo cloned and a Colab notebook ready to run.

annotation at the entity span level, it’s verifiable…

I researched prior work on probing user state and found the area is more developed than I'd assumed.

I'm now rescoping the project to focus on either Sharma's amplifying factors, Boxo-style leakage mitigation, or temporal precedence between user-state probes and downstream behavior. I want to use today's meeting to help decide which direction.

Blocker: more directionally on what

**Distortions (what the model does):**

* Reality distortion: validating false beliefs about the world  
* Value distortion: overriding the user's ethical reasoning  
* Action distortion: scripting the user's actions verbatim

**Amplifying factors (user-state conditions that increase risk):**

* Vulnerability: user in distress or crisis  
* Attachment: user treats AI as a companion  
* Reliance: user depends on AI for daily decisions  
* Authority projection: user treats AI as expert or oracle

Notes from Shivam:

  * maybe assistant axis  
  * steering to increase / decrease the phenomena for the understanding of the user….  
* my mental model of probe “learns an approximate direction”  
  * if you steer a linear probe direction, its not necessarily gonna be coherent …  
* linear probe is a way to partition  
* vs the steering is the mean   
  * different levels of the steering  
* the linear probes depends alot on the input data, so making it as diverse as possible would be cool
